articles.title: Open Thread
articles.content:
登录
创建新账户
主要是法学教授|偶尔反主流|常常自由至上主义|始终保持独立
你在想什么？
尤金·沃洛克
|
2026年7月13日 凌晨3:00
下一篇： Title IX 是否在拖累美国男子国际足球？
尤金·沃洛克是斯坦福大学胡佛研究所的托马斯·西贝尔高级研究员，同时也是加州大学洛杉矶分校法学院荣誉退休的加里·T·施瓦茨杰出法学教授和杰出研究教授。当然，他在这里的帖子（如同其他博主的帖子一样）仅代表个人观点，不代表任何机构的立场。他还是“言论自由不受限制”播客的联合主持人。

articles.title: Is Title IX Holding Back U.S. Men's International Soccer?
articles.content:
在美国男足国家队世界杯失利之后，分析师们一直在思考美国如何在国际比赛中提高人才水平。在1比4负于比利时队的比赛中，其他国家（通常是欧洲国家）在男足人才培养方面显然做得更好。为什么美国没有培养出更好的男足人才？作为美国男足（和女足）国家队的忠实球迷，我一直密切关注这场辩论。通常被指责的原因包括：美国青少年足球的“付费参与”体系、美国最优秀的运动员被分流到其他运动项目，以及据称缺乏真正的美国足球“文化”。

但在上周五，遗产基金会的斯科特·耶诺试图在这个名单上增加一个新的嫌疑因素。他提出了自己的观点，认为美国的第九条法案制度是“美国男足在国际舞台上表现不佳的一个因素”。我不确定他的文章是否主要是为了挑起争议。但这篇文章与当前足球人才培养的现实情况完全脱节，因此有必要做一个简要回应。按照耶诺的说法，第九条法案禁止大学体育项目中的性别歧视，这产生了“扭曲的激励机制”。据耶诺称，第九条法案迫使拥有（美国）橄榄球队的大学通过增加其他项目女子队的名额来平衡——在某些情况下，还削减了男子足球队。耶诺展示的统计数据显示，美国大学体育协会（NCAA）第一级别项目中按性别划分的足球参与人数随着时间推移发生了变化，如今女性人数超过了男性。据耶诺称，这些统计数据表明，“美国女子足球拥有世界级的人才培养体系……第九条法案阻碍了男子足球建立这样的体系。”耶诺挑衅性地总结道：“只有当美国取消体育性别配额制度，美国男足才不会再被比利时羞辱。”

在我看来，耶诺的论点暴露了他对国际男子足球人才培养体系缺乏任何清晰的理解。由于他没有认真参与关于如何改进美国男足国家队的真正辩论，他的政策建议完全偏离了目标。从他的图表开始，他的时间线与世界大赛期间美国男足（和女足）的表现几乎没有关联。第九条法案在1970年代中期生效。在此之前和之后，美国男队都有多年未能获得世界杯参赛资格。聚焦现代时代，美国男队在1954年至1986年间未获得参赛资格。美国男队在1990年获得资格——这是在第九条法案生效十多年之后——并在1994年的下一次机会中进入十六强。2002年，美国男足国家队进入八强。在最近四次参赛——2010年、2014年、2022年和2026年——男队表现稳定，每次都进入十六强……但都没有更进一步。诚然，第九条法案的颁布无疑推动了美国女足的腾飞。她们赢得了1991年第一届女子世界杯冠军，还曾在1999年、2015年和2019年夺冠。但在最近一届2023年世界杯上，美国国家女子足球队表现令人失望，仅进入十六强。这种趋势线——持续、长期的成功之后在最近一届比赛中急剧下降——与耶诺所描述的数十年来女性大学参与人数的逐步增加几乎没有关系。

耶诺论证的核心问题在于他关注的是人才培养体系的最末端——进入大学足球项目的球员。但正如在比利时失利前后被广泛讨论的那样——培养国际级人才需要比大学阶段早得多开始。《The Athletic》有一篇很好的讨论总结。它引用了研究体育和人才培养的荷兰教授玛丽耶·埃尔费林克-格姆泽的话。她解释说，在足球运动中，“球感技能非常困难，你真的需要从小就开始培养。”看起来培养技能获取的“黄金时期”是儿童生命的前六年，尽管有些人可能认为这个年龄可以延长到十二岁。在孩子学会基本的球感技能后，这个过程的下一步是足球技能的强化训练——同样是在男孩（或女孩）进入大学之前。正如足球人才培养辩论中经常讨论的那样，许多欧洲（和其他）国家都有足球“学院”，有潜力的新星从九岁开始接受训练。以世界上最优秀的球员为例：法国的基利安·姆巴佩。姆巴佩六岁时开始在当地俱乐部接受训练。然后他进入了法国国家足球学院——克莱枫丹——在那里令人印象深刻的表现使得许多欧洲职业俱乐部试图签下他。十四岁时，姆巴佩加入了法国足球强队摩纳哥的青年学院。十六岁时，他成为摩纳哥一线队的球员。十八岁时，他以1.8亿欧元的价格完成了转会到法国最好的职业俱乐部球队（巴黎圣日耳曼）。从那以后，他一直是国际公认的明星。要培养顶级国际级人才，美国需要为一些精英有潜力的足球运动员复制这种从小开始的强化专业训练。从人才培养的角度来看，辩论第九条法案的改变是否可能略微提升德克萨斯大学或佛罗里达大学（耶诺引用的例子，那里只有大学俱乐部球队）的男子足球项目是没有意义的。NCAA第一级别已经有超过6000名男子足球运动员。在大学足球替补席上多几个球员不会改变美国在国际足坛的地位。

当然，耶诺假设改变第九条法案以增加美国大学的奖学金名额将产生更多美国球员；但许多获得奖学金的运动员来自其他国家。例如，考虑一下2024年NCAA第一级别男子决赛中佛蒙特大学与马歇尔大学的比赛。根据一项统计，73%的首发球员是来自德国、日本和巴西等国家的国际球员；马歇尔大学的28名球员阵容中只有三人是美国人。通过查看美国世界杯阵容名单，大学参与度对男足国家队重要性的下降很容易看出。诚然，大学足球曾经是美国男足国家队的核心人才渠道：1990年世界杯阵容中的每一名球员似乎都打过美国大学足球，1994年和2002年阵容中约四分之三的球员也是如此。但到了现代时代，这一比例急剧下降。在最近两届世界杯中，似乎只有约8名26名球员曾打过大学足球——而且其中只有少数人是首发。在本月对阵比利时队的比赛中，美国男足国家队的首发阵容中，似乎只有守门员马特·弗里斯（哈佛大学）和38岁的后卫蒂姆·里姆（圣路易斯大学）是前NCAA足球运动员。大学球员减少的趋势可归因于若干与第九条法案对NCAA奖学金规定无关的因素。其中最重要的因素之一是美国职业足球大联盟（MLS）学院在美国崛起，旨在与成熟的欧洲足球学院竞争。美国有二十多支基于MLS的球队。所有球队都有强大的学院体系来培养本地人才，要么补充球队阵容，要么通过在国际转会市场上出售这些球员来创造收入。美国的下一代人才很可能来自这些学院。《The Athletic》已经对哪些新球员最有可能入选2030年美国男足国家队世界杯阵容做出了有趣预测。假设14名现有球员回归，那么就有可能预测额外的16名新球员，分为“险些入选”（第15-20名）和“下一代”（第21-30名）类别，并列出年龄和当前所属俱乐部：

2026年险些入选
15. 诺亚凯·班克斯（19岁）——奥格斯堡足球俱乐部
16. 约翰尼·卡多索（24岁）——马德里竞技
17. 坦纳·特斯曼（24岁）——里昂奥林匹克
18. 艾丹·莫里斯（24岁）——米德尔斯堡足球俱乐部
19. 帕特里克·阿吉曼（25岁）——德比郡
20. 迭戈·卢纳（22岁）——皇家盐湖城

2030年下一代
21. 扎维耶·戈佐（19岁）——皇家盐湖城
22. 卡万·沙利文（16岁）——费城联合
23. 阿德里·梅赫梅蒂（17岁）——纽约红牛
24. 朱利安·霍尔（18岁）——纽约红牛
25. 佩顿·米勒（18岁）——新英格兰革命
26. 马蒂斯·艾伯特（17岁）——多特蒙德
27. 罗卡斯·普克斯塔斯（21岁）——斯普利特海杜克
28. 朱利安·艾斯通（20岁）——布伦特福德
29. 迭戈·科亨（20岁）——灵比足球俱乐部（租借自巴塞罗那）
30. 乔什·温德（21岁）——本菲卡（B队）

在这份16名潜在球员名单中，似乎只有三人之前打过大学足球（或似乎未来可能会打）：艾丹·莫里斯、帕特里克·阿吉曼、朱利安·艾斯通。值得注意的是，这三人中只有两人也曾在MLS学院待过（莫里斯，在哥伦布机员学院度过了进入大学前的三年；艾斯通，约十岁时加入了达拉斯FC学院）。而这三人中只有一人——阿吉曼，名单上年龄最大的球员——打了四年大学足球。莫里斯在印第安纳大学打了一个秋季赛季后离开，与哥伦布机员签约；艾斯通在杜克大学打了一个秋季赛季后，与英格兰的布伦特福德签约。

美国男子足球运动员（如莫里斯和艾斯通）想要尽快离开NCAA足球项目的原因之一是，NCAA要求运动员首先是学生，其次才是足球运动员。一般来说，NCAA规则限制了“可计入的体育相关活动”数量。在比赛季，标准第一级别限制是每天4小时，每周20小时，并有强制休息日。而且赛季每年只有几个月。对于精英足球人才来说，NCAA规则是重大限制。职业环境可以提供专业性：全年每日训练、个性化技术训练、力量和体能训练、恢复、营养、录像分析、预备队比赛和一线队融合。NCAA赛季被压缩在约三个月的秋季窗口期，有时会出现八天内三场比赛的安排——这是一个糟糕的训练计划。还有其他问题。但总体观点是，随着越来越少美国精英男性进入大学足球项目，这可能会通过让他们处于更具竞争力和专业性的环境中来改善人才储备。

关于非大学途径进入美国男足国家队这一现象的出色学术分析来自萨姆福德大学体育分析中心。在今年5月发布的一份报告中，该中心预见性地预测了美国男足国家队在7月进入十六强后被淘汰。该报告的标题是《2026年美国男足国家队是美国历史上最好的美国足球队，但它仍然不够好》。该报告仔细解释了，通过比较1994年和2026年的美国男足国家队，如今更少的球员来自大学足球。相反，除了历史性的大学途径外，还存在四条进入美国男足国家队的其他途径：（1）青年出口；（2）MLS学院到外国联赛；（3）MLS“终身制”；（4）在外国成长的双重国籍球员。而且有趣的是，报告指出，“在2026年美国男足国家队中，你越往场地前方看，产生球员的体系就越欧洲化。”该报告解释说，目前即使是MLS也没有培养出美国决定比赛胜负的球员。



欧洲俱乐部正在做的事情是，美国青少年如今正与全世界最优秀的年轻球员竞争Academy名额。这份报告还令人印象深刻地展示了详细的数据分析，表明预测一支国家足球队在世界杯上表现如何的关键指标，是一个国家的球员在刚刚结束的这个赛季中，在五大欧洲顶级足球联赛中的总出场时间。按照这个指标，美国目前仅排在前十之外（第十位）——恰好与该队在今年世界杯上的最终排名一致。因此，在男足方面，大学培养体系对世界杯成绩几乎没有影响。但女足方面呢？耶诺尔也认为，与美国男足相比，美国女足通过大学足球建立了“世界级的培养通道”。可以肯定的是，20世纪70年代通过的《教育法修正案第九条》帮助推动了美国女足的崛起。这一时机使美国女子足球比其他国家更早起步。但在这里，耶诺尔的图表（见上图复制）与那次起步几乎没有关联。该图表显示了过去几十年NCAA第一级别联赛中女子参赛人数的增加。但美国国家女子足球队（USWNT）的球员培养始终集中在少数几所精英大学项目中。在大学层面培养国际级女子足球运动员始终高度集中在最具竞争力的学校。最典型的例子是北卡罗来纳大学（UNC），那里令人惊叹的焦油踵女子项目赢得了最初19届全国冠军中的16届。最近，斯坦福大学、圣克拉拉大学、宾夕法尼亚州立大学、圣母大学等学校也参与其中。但越来越多的美国国家女子足球队球员选择转职业，从未打过大学联赛。避开NCAA足球的趋势是由于最近法律环境的变化，现在允许优秀的美国女孩在很小的时候就转为职业球员。奥利维亚·莫尔特里是这方面的先驱。莫尔特里四岁时开始踢足球。到五年级时，她开始在家自学，以便专注于足球，并成为美国足球发展学院体系中首位参加男子俱乐部球队的女孩。11岁时，她接受了北卡罗来纳大学焦油踵队提供的全额奖学金，以便在达到大学年龄时为该校效力，成为当时公开接受大学邀请的最年轻的女足球运动员。此后不久，她前往欧洲训练。13岁时，她开始在波特兰荆棘队（属于美国女子足球职业联赛NWSL）训练。2021年，15岁的莫尔特里在俄勒冈州联邦地区法院对NWSL提起反垄断诉讼。她指控NWSL作为职业市场“唯一的球员收购方”，其要求球员必须年满18岁的年龄限制违反了《谢尔曼反垄断法》。她的诉讼请求法院对年龄限制发出临时限制令，以便她能够参加2021年职业联赛。美国联邦地区法官卡琳·伊梅尔古特批准了临时限制令和初步禁令，命令该联赛解除年龄限制，允许莫尔特里竞争荆棘队的大名单名额。莫尔特里成为NWSL历史上最年轻的进球球员和参加NWSL championship比赛最年轻的球员。毫无疑问，莫尔特里也曾入选美国国家女子足球队，共出场18次，其中2026年出场7次。莫尔特里绝非走这条职业道路的唯一一人。最近，美国许多最耀眼的国家女子足球队新星都跳过了大学。考虑一下明年在巴西举办的2027年女子世界杯上美国女子队可能的首发阵容，按年龄从大到小排列，并注明任何大学经历：

在这份名单的11名球员中，只有5人打过NCAA足球，其中只有4人打满了四年。而且老将和年轻球员之间的差异非常明显：在这份可能的首发名单中，最年轻的5名球员（如莫尔特里），都没有打过NCAA足球。与男足一样，关于女足的最新学术研究强调了这些非大学培养通道对美国国家女子足球队的重要性。鉴于其他国家女子足球队的崛起——显然是由职业俱乐部提供的职业训练机会推动的——对这些非大学培养通道的需求正在增加。美国女队在2023年上届世界杯上的成绩是历届最差的——止步于十六强。需要新一代接受过职业训练的球员来使美国重返巅峰。正如顶级球员加入美国男足和女足国家队的近期培养通道所表明的那样，NCAA大学足球作为国际人才培养渠道的重要性正在逐渐消失。或许有人会提出修改《教育法修正案第九条》某些规则的论点，以扩大男性在足球或其他运动项目中的体育机会。（我在此不就此问题表态。）但声称修改《教育法修正案第九条》的规则能对美国男足国家队产生任何有意义的好处的说法，不过是一个具有煽动性的干扰，转移了人们对真正问题的注意力。要击败最优秀的职业球员，美国最优秀的男足和女足球运动员需要走最专业的道路。

注：为简洁起见，在本文中我用“世界杯”和“女子世界杯”，因为这是国际足联给出的官方赛事名称。我承认，对女子比赛使用性别修饰词而对男子比赛不使用，可能被视为将女子比赛降为次等地位。但目前，这种语言方式似乎是惯例。

更新：我已更正了2027年美国国家女子足球队首发名单中的一个令人尴尬的错误，换上了守门员法珑·图利斯-乔伊斯。感谢大卫·尼波伦特发现了我这个尴尬的名单投影错误。这一更正意味着，2027年美国国家女子足球队的首发阵容中有5人打过NCAA第一级别联赛。（作为犹他皇家队的球迷，我希望曼迪·麦克格林能获得守门员位置。她就读于弗吉尼亚理工大学，2016-2019年。）

进一步更新：我已更正了威廉·布鲁克林等人指出的几处小打字错误。《教育法修正案第九条是否阻碍了美国男子国际足球？》一文首次发表于Reason.com。
articles.summary:
In the wake of the World Cup soccer defeat of the U.S. Men's National Team (USMNT), analysts have  been wondering what the U.S. can do better to improve its talent in international competitions. In the 4-1 defeat to Belgium, it seemed apparent that other countries (often European countries) have done a better job of developing a talent pipeline for men's soccer than the U.S. Why hasn't the U.S. developed better men's talent?

As an avid fan of the U.S. men's (and women's) national soccer teams, I have been following the debate closely. Commonly identified culprits include: America's "pay to play" system of youth soccer, diversion of some of America's best athletes to other sports, and the alleged lack of a true American soccer "culture."

But on Friday, Scott Yenor of the Heritage Foundation tried to add a new suspect to the list. He offered his view that America's Title IX regime was "a factor behind the underperformance of U.S. men's soccer on the international stage." I'm not sure whether his article was intended mainly to provoke. But the article is so disassociated from the current realities surrounding soccer talent development that a brief response may be warranted.

As Yenor tells the tale, Title IX's prohibition of sex-based discrimination in college sports programs has created "perverse incentives." According to Yenor, Title IX forces universities with (American) football teams to balance things out by padding the rosters of their women's teams in other sports—and, in some cases, cutting men's soccer teams. Yenor displays statistics showing that soccer participation by sex in National Collegiate Athletic Association (NCAA) Division I programs has changed over time, so that women now outnumber men.

According to Yenor, these statistics show that, while "America's women's soccer has a world-class pipeline … Title IX prevents men's soccer from building one." Yenor provocatively concludes that "[o]nly when America drops its sports sex-quota system will the American men stop being humiliated by Belgium."

In my view, Yenor's argument betrays a lack of any clear understanding of how talent pipelines of men in international soccer work. Because he does not seriously engage with the real debate over how to improve the USMNT, his policy recommendation misses the mark.

Starting with his graph, his timeline shows little connection to the World Cup performances by America's men's (and women's) national teams. Title IX became effective in the mid-1970s. The men's team did not qualify to participate in any World Cups for many years both before and after. Focusing on the modern era, the U.S. men did not qualify to play between 1954 and 1986. The U.S. men did qualify in 1990—more than a decade after Title IX went into effect—and reached the Round of 16 at the next opportunity, in 1994. In 2002, the USMNT reached the quarterfinals.  In its last four appearances—in 2010, 2014, 2022, and 2026—the men played consistently, reached the Round of 16 each time … but progressed no further.

To be sure, Title IX's enactment no doubt helped propel the U.S. women to success. They won the first women's World Cup in 1991. They were also champions in 1999, 2015, and 2019. But in its most recent appearance, in 2023, the U.S. Women's National Team (USWNT) disappointingly only reached the Round of 16. This trendline—consistent, long-standing success followed by a sharp drop in the most recent tournament—bears little relation to the escalating college participation over decades by women that Yenor depicts.

The core problem with Yenor's argument is its focus on the very backend of the talent pipeline—players entering college soccer programs. But as has been widely discussed—both before and after the USMNT's defeat against Belgium—developing international-level talent requires starting much earlier than college. The Athletic has a good summary of the discussion. It quotes Marije Elferink-Gemser (a Dutch professor who studies sports and talent development). She explains that, in soccer, "the ball-handling skills are so difficult that you really need to do it from a young age on." It appears that "golden age" for developing skill acquisition is the first six years of a child's life, although some might argue that the age extends all the way to age twelve.

After a child learns basic ball handling skills, the next step in the process is intensive training in soccer skills—again, well before a boy (or girl) enters college. As is commonly discussed in the soccer talent debate, many European (and other) countries have soccer "academies," where budding stars begin training as young as age nine. As an example, consider perhaps the best player in the world: France's Kylian Mbappé. Mbappé began training with his local club at age six. Then he moved to the French national football academy—Clairefontaine—where his impressive performances led to many professional European teams trying to sign him. At age 14, Mbappé joined the youth academy of the French soccer power, Monaco. By age 16, he became a first-team player for Monaco. At age 18, he completed a move to France's best professional club team (Paris Saint-Germain) for a price of €180 million. Ever since he has been an internationally recognized star.

To develop top-tier, international-level talent, the U.S. is going to need to replicate this kind of intensive professional training at a young age for some of its elite, budding soccer players. From a talent-pipeline perspective, it makes no sense to debate whether changes in Title IX might slightly upgrade the men's soccer programs at the University of Texas or University of Florida (examples cited by Yenor, where only university club teams exist). There are already more than 6,000 men's soccer players playing in NCAA Division I. A few more players at the end of the bench in college soccer isn't going to change America's international standing.

And, of course, Yenor is assuming that changing Title IX to add additional scholarships at U.S. universities will result in more American players; but many scholarship athletes come from other countries. As one example, consider the 2024 NCAA Division I men's final between Vermont and Marshall. According to one tabulation, 73% of the starters were international players from countries such as Germany, Japan, and Brazil; Marshall's squad of 28 players contained only three who were American.

The declining importance of college participation to the USMNT is easy to see by looking at U.S. World Cup rosters. To be sure, college soccer was once the central pipeline for the U.S. men's national team: every player on the 1990 World Cup roster appears to have played U.S. college soccer, and roughly three-quarters of the 1994 and 2002 rosters did. But by the modern era, that share had fallen sharply. In the last two World Cups, it appears that only about 8 of the 26 players had done so—and only a few of them were starters. Of the USMNT starters on the field against Belgium this month, only goalkeeper Matt Freese (Harvard) and 38-year-old defender Tim Ream (Saint Louis) appear to have been former NCAA soccer players.

The trend towards fewer ex-college players is attributable to several factors that have nothing to do with Title IX regulations on NCAA scholarships. One of the most important factors is the rise of Major League Soccer (MLS) academies in the U.S., designed to compete with the well-developed European soccer academies. More than two dozen U.S.-based MLS teams exist. All teams have strong academies designed to develop local talent, either to supplement their rosters or to generate income by selling these players in the international transfer market.

The next generation of U.S. talent may well come from these academies. The Athletic already has an interesting projection of which new players are most likely to make the 2030 World Cup roster of the USMNT. Assuming that 14 current players return, it is then possible to project an additional 16 new arrivals, broken into the categories of "just missed" (#15-20) and "next generation" (#21-30), with ages and current club affiliation listed:

Just Missed in 2026

15. Noahkai Banks (19) — FC Augsburg
16. Johnny Cardoso (24) — Atlético de Madrid
17. Tanner Tessmann (24) — Olympique Lyon
18. Aidan Morris (24) — Middlesbrough FC
19. Patrick Agyemang (25) — Derby County
20. Diego Luna (22) — Real Salt Lake

Next Generation in 2030

21. Zavier Gozo (19) — Real Salt Lake
22. Cavan Sullivan (16) — Philadelphia Union
23. Adri Mehmeti (17) — New York Red Bulls
24. Julian Hall (18) — New York Red Bulls
25. Peyton Miller (18) — New England Revolution
26. Mathis Albert (17) — Borussia Dortmund
27. Rokas Pukstas (21) — HNK Hajduk Split
28. Julian Eyestone (20) — Brentford
29. Diego Kochen (20) — Lyngby Boldklub, on loan from FC Barcelona
30. Josh Wynder (21) — SL Benfica (B team)

Of this list of sixteen potential players, only three appear to have previously played college soccer (or seem likely to do so in the future): Aidan Morris, Patrick Agyemang, Julian Eyestone. Notably, two of these three also went to an MLS academy (Morris, who spent three pre-college years at the Columbus Crew academy; and Eyestone, who joined the FC Dallas academy at around age 10). And only one of the three — Agyemang, the oldest player on the list — played four years of college soccer. Morris played one fall season at Indiana, before leaving to sign with the Columbus Crew; Eyestone played one fall season at Duke, before signing with Brentford in England. By my count, eleven of the sixteen went to MLS academies and several of the others went to foreign academies (Banks at FC Augsburg, Cardoso at Brazilian academies, and Kochen at Barcelona's famous La Masia academy) and Josh Wynder went to a Louisville City/USL academy (the second-tier league below MLS).

Part of the reason that U.S. men's players (such as Morris and Eyestone) want to leave NCAA soccer programs quickly is that the NCAA requires that athletes be students first, and soccer players second. Generally speaking, the NCAA rules cap the amount of "countable athletically related activities." During the playing season, the standard Division I limit is 4 hours per day and 20 hours per week, with required days off. And the season lasts only a few months of the year.

There are occasional NCAA exceptions allowing more training for Olympic-level athletes. But the last thing that American soccer would want to encourage is its top athletes going into NCAA-regulated college soccer programs. For an elite soccer talent, the NCAA rules are major constraints. A professional environment can offer, well, professionalism: such things as daily training throughout the year, individualized technical work, strength and conditioning, recovery, nutrition, film study, reserve-team matches, and first-team integration. The NCAA season is compressed in a fall window of about three months, with some stretches of three games in eight days—a bad program for training. There are other problems as well. But the general point is that as fewer elite American men go into college soccer programs, that likely improves our talent pool by putting them in more competitive and professional environments.

An excellent scholarly analysis of the rise in non-college pathways to the USMNT comes from Samford University's Center for Sports Analytics. In a report published this May, the Center presciently predicted something like the USMNT's round-of-16 exit in July. The report's title is "The 2026 USMNT Is the Best American Soccer Team in History. It Still Isn't Good Enough." The report carefully explains, comparing the 1994 and 2026 USMNTs, far fewer of today's players are arriving from college soccer. Instead, four additional pathways, apart from the historical college path, exist to arrive at the USMNT: (1) the Youth Export, (2) the MLS-Academy-to-Foreign-League; (3) the MLS "Lifer"; and (4) the  Foreign-Raised Dual-National.

And, interestingly, the report notes that the "further up the pitch [i.e., field] you go on the 2026 USMNT, the more European the system that produced the player becomes." The report explains that, currently, even the MLS does not produce America's match-deciding players. European clubs do … and American teenagers are now competing for academy spots at those clubs alongside the world's best young players. The report also impressively presents detailed analytics, showing that the key metric for predicting how a national soccer team will perform at the World Cup is total playing time for a country's nationals in the five elite European soccer leagues over the just-completed season. By that metric, the U.S. currently sits just outside the top eight teams (in tenth position)—exactly where the team finished in this year's World Cup.

So on the men's side, the college pipeline has little relevance to World Cup performance. But what about the women's side? Yenor also argues that, compared to the U.S. men, the U.S. women have built a "world-class pipeline" through college soccer. To be sure, Title IX's enactment in the 1970s helped to propel the U.S. women's team to the top. The timing gave American women's soccer a jump start ahead of other countries.

But here again, Yenor's chart (reproduced above) bears little connection to that jump start. The chart shows increasing women's participation in NCAA Division I over the last several decades. But the development of players for the USWNT has always been heavily concentrated in a handful of elite college programs. The fact that (for example) such schools as Mercyhurst University, Lindenwood University, and others have recently added Division I women's soccer programs is disconnected from the national team program.

NCAA development of international-level women's soccer players has always been heavily concentrated in the most competitive schools. The prime example is UNC, where the amazing Tarheels women's program won 16 of the first 19 national titles. More recently, schools like Stanford, Santa Clara, Penn State, Notre Dame, and a few others have been involved. But increasingly USWNT players are turning pro and never playing college soccer.

The trend of avoiding NCAA soccer is due to a recent change in the legal environment that now allows strong American girls to turn professional at a young age. Olivia Moultrie was the trailblazer here. Moultrie started playing soccer when she was four years old. By fifth grade, she was homeschooled so that she could focus on soccer and became the first girl on a boys' club team to play in the U.S. Soccer Development Academy system. At 11, she accepted a full scholarship offer to play soccer for the UNC Tar Heels when she reached college age, becoming the youngest female soccer player to publicly accept a college offer at the time. Shortly thereafter, she traveled to Europe to train. When she was 13, she began training with the Portland Thorns (of the National Women's Soccer League or "NWSL").

In 2021, when she was 15, Moultrie filed an antitrust lawsuit against the NWSL in district court in Oregon. She alleged that the NWSL, as the "only acquirer of talent in the [professional] market," violated the Sherman Antitrust Act with its age-requirement that players be 18 years old. Her lawsuit asked for a temporary restraining order against the age rule so that she could play during the 2021 pro season. U.S. District Judge Karin Immergut granted a temporary restraining order and preliminary injunction, ordering the league to lift its age limit and allow Moultrie to compete for a roster spot on the Thorns. Moultrie became the youngest player in NWSL history to score a goal and appear in a NWSL championship game.

Unsurprisingly, Moultrie has also featured for the USWNT, where she has made a total of 18 appearances, including 7 during 2026. And Moultrie is hardly alone in taking this professional path. Recently, many of America's brightest young USWNT stars have skipped college. Consider what a projected "starting eleven" lineup for the U.S. women's team might look like next year in the 2027 Women's World Cup in Brazil, arranged from oldest to youngest player, with any college experience noted:

  1. Emily Fox (28 years old) — North Carolina, 2017-2020.
  2. Mallory Swanson/Pugh (28) — Enrolled at UCLA but left before playing an official NCAA season; no NCAA soccer.
  3. Phallon Tullis-Joyce (27) — Univ. of Miami, 2014-18.
  4. Tierna Davidson (27) — Stanford, 2016-2018.
  5. Naomi Girma (26)— Stanford, 2018-2021.
  6. Sophia Wilson/Smith (25) —Stanford, 2018-2019; only two NCAA seasons.
  7. Trinity Rodman (24) — Turned pro directly; no NCAA soccer.
  8. Jaedyn Shaw (21) — Turned pro at 17 rather than playing college soccer; no NCAA soccer.
  9. Gisele Thompson (20) — Signed directly with Angel City rather than playing at Stanford; no NCAA soccer.
  10. Olivia Moultrie (20) — Turned pro with Portland; NWSL debut at 15;  no NCAA soccer.
  11. Lily Yohannes (19) — Ajax/Lyon pro pathway; no NCAA soccer.

Of the eleven players on this list, only five played NCAA soccer, and only four played for the full four years. And the difference between the older players and younger players is stark: Of the five youngest players on the possible projected starter list (such as Moultrie), none played NCAA soccer.

As with the men's team, recent scholarly research about the women's team highlights the importance of these non-college pathways to the USWNT. And given the rising strength of women's soccer teams in other countries—apparently fueled by professional training opportunities at professional clubs—the need for these non-college pathways is increasing. The U.S. women exited the last World Cup in 2023 earlier than ever before—in the Round of 16. A new generation of professionally trained players is needed to return America to the top.

As the recent pathways for top players joining both the U.S. men's and women's national teams demonstrate, NCAA college soccer is fading into obscurity as part of the international talent pipeline. There may be arguments for modifying some of Title IX's rules to expand athletic opportunities for men in soccer or other sports. (I take no position on that issue here.) But the claim that changing Title IX's rules could have any meaningful benefit for the American men's international soccer team is but a provocative diversion from the real issues. To beat the best professionals, the top U.S. men—and women—need to follow the most professional paths.

***

Note: In the interests of brevity, in this post I have referred to the "World Cup" and "Women's World Cup," because those are the official names of the competitions as given by FIFA. I acknowledge that using the sex modifier for the women's game but not the men's might be viewed as relegating the women's game to second-class status. But, at this time, this linguistic approach appears to be conventional.

Update: I've corrected an embarrassing mistake on my list of projected 2027 USWNT starters, swapping in goalkeeper Phallon Tullis-Joyce. Thanks to David Nieporent for catching my embarrassing roster projection error. That correction means that five of the projected starters for the USWNT in 2027 played NCAA Division I soccer. (As a fan of the Utah Royals, I hope that Mandy McGlynn secures the goalkeeper spot. She played for Virginia Tech, 2016-19.)

Further update: I've correct a couple of small typos pointed out by William of Brooklyn and others.

The post Is Title IX Holding Back U.S. Men's International Soccer? appeared first on Reason.com.

沃尔洛赫阴谋博客

articles.title: Andy Serkis on Animal Farm, Authoritarianism, and Humanizing Monsters
articles.content:
安迪·瑟金斯（Andy Serkis）塑造过当代电影史上一些最令人难忘的角色：《指环王》和《霍比特人》三部曲中的咕噜、《金刚》中的金刚、《猩球崛起》系列中的凯撒、传记片《性与毒品与摇滚乐》中的原型朋克偶像伊恩·杜里（Ian Dury），以及漫威电影宇宙中的军火商尤利西斯·克劳（Ulysses Klaue）。

瑟金斯也已转型幕后：他执导了改编自乔治·奥威尔（George Orwell）小说《动物庄园》的动画电影，该片于5月1日在影院上映。当月下旬，瑟金斯接受了《理性》（Reason）杂志尼克·吉尔斯皮（Nick Gillespie）的采访，谈论他的新片、威权主义，以及他标志性角色之间的共同点。

问：让我们谈谈《动物庄园》。是什么吸引你接触这个题材？

答：我在十岁或十一岁时，在乘公交车上学的路上读了这本书。那个年纪的孩子开始读一些他们知道蕴含深层主题的书，但并不真正理解其中暗含的深意。那本书有一种纯真感，但我能感受到其下有一个隐藏的世界。这是一本从未离开过我的书。后来在人生的不同阶段，我又多次重读。

问：这是一个改编作品，也是对原著的更新。你所批判的体制并非社会主义或国际共产主义。你的切入点是什么？

答：不是。当我们开始创作时，我们与奥威尔遗产管理方进行了大量讨论。如果奥威尔现在写这个故事，他的目标会是什么？他是一个在更高层面上憎恶威权主义的人。在那个特定时期，他的矛头指向一个特定的政权。但如果奥威尔现在写《动物庄园》，他的目标会是什么？

很多主题完全相同。只是当今世界各地使用威权主义和独裁来维持生存的不同管理体系显然正在蓬勃发展。这不是针对任何一位领导人的讽刺寓言。我们不是说"这是某个政治领导人"，而是在批判所有控制我们的框架和世界各地的威权现象，比如信息滥用、假新闻——这些都是书中涉及的主题，以及权力的腐败。我们是在用现代视角看待这些，面向年轻观众，这一点至关重要。它本来就是作为面向儿童的寓言来写的，就像我那个年纪读到的一样。

问：奥威尔80年前创作《动物庄园》，当时威权主义在各地似乎正在兴起。与那时相比，我们的处境是否更好？

答：我并不认为我们的处境更好。这个故事的部分主旨是关于历史不断重演，以及为什么我们总是犯同样的错误。即使我们确实在重蹈覆辙，也不应停止尝试纠正这些错误。这确实是我们这个版本的核心：没有标准答案，我们可能会继续重复同样的事情，但你仍然必须不断尝试去理解。

这确实关乎学会倾听。制作这个版本的意义之一——它是让年轻人与父母和祖父母坐在一起观看的电影，这些人可能对世界有着截然不同的观点，然后真正开始通过辩论来探究。目的是引发讨论，但这是一部家庭电影，所以希望能激发跨代际的辩论。

问：你参与过当代电影界一些最成功的系列电影。你扮演的角色是否有共同主线？咕噜是否体现了被更多权力的可能性所腐蚀的人？这是否是你在许多角色中追逐的阴影自我？

答：演员或讲故事者的工作是尝试拓宽观众的视角。如果你作为演员深入一个角色的内心，你必须相信那个角色有其信奉的道德观。我扮演的每一个角色，我都想要审视这一点，因此作为一个人，你需要对那些你未必想去的地方产生共情。那是表演的挑战——你无法否认，因为你必须相信那个角色从他的视角看待世界就是正确的视角。我们将怪物在一定程度上人性化，以便能够理解它们，并希望从中学习。

本次采访已经过压缩和编辑，仅为风格和清晰度之需。

本文《安迪·瑟金斯谈〈动物庄园〉、威权主义和人性化怪物》首次发表于Reason.com。
articles.summary:
:15 :15
Download

Andy Serkis: What Orwell Understood About Tyranny

Andy Serkis has played some of contemporary film's most memorable characters: Gollum in the Lord of the Rings and Hobbit trilogies, Kong in King Kong, Caesar in the Planet of the Apes reboot, proto-punk icon Ian Dury in the biopic Sex & Drugs & Rock & Roll, and arms dealer Ulysses Klaue in the Marvel Cinematic Universe.

Serkis has also moved behind the camera: He directed an animated adaptation of George Orwell's Animal Farm, which was released in theaters on May 1. Later that month, Serkis spoke with Reason's Nick Gillespie about his new film, authoritarianism, and what unites his signature roles.

Q: Let's talk about Animal Farm. What drew you to the material?

A: I read the book when I was about 10 or 11 years old on the bus to school. You get to that age and you start reading things that you know have underlying themes, but you don't really fully understand what are being driven at. It had an innocence about it, and yet I could feel that there was a world that was underneath it. It was one of those books that just never left me. I read it again in years to come at various different points of my life.

Q: This is an adaptation and an update of the original. The system that you're critiquing is not socialism or international communism. Where do you go with that?

A: No. When we started working on it, we talked to the Orwell estate a lot about it. If Orwell were writing that story now, what would his targets be? He was someone who reviled authoritarianism on a grander level. At that particular time, he was aiming at a particular regime. But if he was writing Animal Farm right now, what would be his targets?

A lot of the themes are exactly the same. It's just the different management systems around the world that use authoritarianism and dictatorship to survive obviously are thriving. This is not a stab at any one leader in terms of the allegory. We're not saying "this is this political leader," but it's having a go at all of the framework of what controls us and what is authoritarian around the world, like the misuse of information, fake news, all of those things which are themes in the book, the corruption of power. It was looking at that in a modern context for a young audience, which is crucial. It was written as an allegory for young children to read, as I read it when I was that age.

Q: Orwell was writing Animal Farm 80 years ago, at a moment when authoritarianism seemed to be on the rise in various places. Compared to then, are we in a better place?

A: I don't necessarily think we are in a better place. Part of this story is about history repeating itself and why it is that we always make the same mistakes. Even though we do, we mustn't stop trying to rectify those mistakes. That's really at the core of our version: There is no answer, and we will probably keep repeating things, but you still have to keep trying to understand.

It really is about learning to listen. One of the things about making this version—it was for young people to sit in a room with their parents and grandparents, who have probably got all different viewpoints about the world, and actually really start to investigate through a debate. It was to cause a debate, but it's a family film, so that will hopefully inspire debate across generations.

Q: You've been part of some of the most successful franchises in contemporary film. Is there a common thread in the roles you've played? Is Gollum the personification of someone who gets corrupted by the possibility of more power? Is that the shadow self that you're chasing in many of these roles?

A: The job of the actor or the storyteller is to try and broaden the audience's perspective. If you're an actor inside the head of a character, you have to believe that there's a morality that that character believes in. Every character I've played, I've wanted to examine that, and therefore it takes you as a person to be empathetic to perhaps places you don't necessarily want to go. That is the challenge of acting—and you can't deny that, because you have to believe that that character sees the world from his perspective as the right view. We humanize monsters to a certain extent in order to be able to understand them and hopefully learn from them.

This interview has been condensed and edited for style and clarity.

The post Andy Serkis on <i>Animal Farm</i>, Authoritarianism, and Humanizing Monsters appeared first on Reason.com.

articles.title: Florida Politician (Anthony F. Sabatini) Faulted for AI Hallucinations in Briefs
articles.content:
Akerlund诉Atlas Air, Inc.案，由第十一巡回法院于昨日作出判决，布里特·格兰特法官主笔，罗宾·罗森鲍姆法官和恩布里·基德法官参与合议：

一群原告是商业航空业的雇员，他们个人反对各公司在大流行期间制定的口罩、检测和疫苗接种政策。地区法院以缺乏对人管辖权及未能陈述诉因为由驳回了第三次修改的诉状，我们维持原判。

本案诉因，至少从诉状所述来看，相当薄弱。我们比平时更加坦率地作出这一评价，因为原告律师安东尼·F·萨巴蒂尼对我们并不坦诚。萨巴蒂尼提交了多份充满虚假和臆造引用的简状。即使在受到警告之后也是如此。"这总是个坏主意。"首席大法官小约翰·G·罗伯茨，2023年联邦司法年度报告，第6页（2023年）。通过将法律工作外包给人工智能算法，萨巴蒂尼违反了他对当事人和本法院的职业道德义务……

尽管本案提交的文件在多方面都不合格[我从下文中摘录了一些实质性分析——EV]，我们把最严重的问题留到最后。原告律师安东尼·F·萨巴蒂尼提交的文件中充满了不存在的、"臆造"的案例。他在开场简状中依赖了至少八个这样的案例，其中包括一个据称由本院作出的判决。

在被告指出这一问题后，萨巴蒂尼在他（逾期提交的）建议答辩简状中承认这些引用是"错误的或无法核实的"，并试图撤回对所列八个案例的依赖。事情从这里开始从糟糕变得更糟：萨巴蒂尼"撤回"的八个案例与他开场简状中的八个臆造案例没有一个相匹配。而且不仅不是正确的案例——这八个案例也全部是臆造的。

我们远非第一个看到律师不加批判地依赖人工智能软件并提交引用不存在案例的简状的法院。当这类违规行为被发现时，典型的回应是律师道歉的某种版本，通常随后会伴随着制裁。但像萨巴蒂尼在本案中犯下的公然且反复的不当行为是罕见的。

我们职业的第一条规则是，律师"应向当事人提供称职的代理"——"称职"意味着需要"法律知识、技能、彻底性和准备工作"。不言而喻，将法律工作完全外包给人工智能软件并不是称职的。这样做严重损害了当事人的利益。

但问题不仅于此，因为律师同时也是法院的官员。我们期望律师提交简状时，能够提供他们对法律的最好理解，以及该法律如何支持其当事人的主张。我们仔细阅读这些简状——不仅因为它们是律师时间、精力和技能的产物，更因为它们帮助我们在寻求正确答案的过程中前行。如果律师认为不值得付出努力来说服我们，所有这些都将失去——如果他愿意在没有任何检查准确性的情况下，在机器生成的文件上签名。我们无法在律师不履行其职责的情况下履行我们的职责。

无论人工智能的优点是什么，它都不是真正智能的替代品。任何"使用人工智能都需要谨慎和谦逊"。当律师依赖人工智能工具时，无法回避验证软件是否正确的义务，而软件出错的内容不会最终出现在法庭文件中。

[人工智能算法以产生"反映用户意见和信仰的输出而闻名，即使这些观点是错误的"而著称。这种倾向在法律文件中构成了特殊危险，因为律师的主要职责之一是给当事人提供对法律是否支持当事人诉求的清醒认识。]

通过在充满臆造引用的简状上签名——不是一次而是两次——萨巴蒂尼违反了他的职业义务，无论是对当事人还是对本法院。如果他认为这些诉因不值得付出努力，他应该要么告诉当事人这些主张站不住脚，要么建议他们聘请另一位律师——而不是提交明显不充分的、由人工智能工具生成的诉状和简状。在单独的命令中，本法院将通过首席法官将此事转交律师资格和行为委员会处理。

《奥兰多哨兵报》（安妮·马丁）报道，萨巴蒂尼是佛罗里达州莱克县的民选县专员，曾任州众议员：

这位37岁的人士多年来因其争议性且往往是侮辱性的社交媒体帖子而成为新闻焦点，甚至在2019年参加第一次立法会议之前，就被塔拉哈西的共和党领导人警告要收敛其言论。

过去，他曾发布一张将AR-15步枪对准乔治·弗洛伊德抗议者的照片，呼吁终结同性婚姻，并且在四年前竞选美国众议院议员失败时，在X平台上发帖称："我竞选国会议员是为了将尽可能多的民主党人送进监狱。"

以下是一些与案件实体相关的摘录：

Atlas Air是一家商业航空公司，Flight Services International雇佣承包商为Atlas Air的航班配备人员。在新冠疫情期间，两家公司都要求雇员接种新冠疫苗，除非他们获得宗教或医疗豁免——在这种情况下，他们必须定期接受检测并在工作时佩戴口罩。

原告称，他们基于宗教理由反对他们所认为的"一项危险的社會和医学实验"。他们解释说，除其他事项外，他们的"良心禁止他们接种任何实验性外来物质"，他们的宗教权利因"拜登政府实现普遍疫苗接种的目标以及非法获取[他们的]个人基因信息"而受到侵犯。在他们看来，公司为宗教反对者提供的疫苗接种要求豁免安排是不合理的：每月检测施加了"沉重负担"，而口罩是"功能上无用"的"象征"，除了"政治宣传"外没有任何作用。一些原告表示，他们"在压力下屈服"接种了疫苗；其他人则不情愿地佩戴口罩和接受检测。诉状未声称任何人被解雇，但确实声称一些原告被分配到不太理想、薪酬更低的航班。

原告主张，雇主的Covid-19协议导致根据《民权法》第七章构成敌意工作环境；违反《联邦食品、药品和化妆品法》；联邦宪法权利被剥夺；侵权性侵犯隐私；疏忽披露私人医疗信息；以及故意造成精神损害。地区法院以缺乏对人管辖权驳回了针对Flight Services International的全部诉请以及针对Atlas Air的部分诉请，并对Atlas Air的其余诉请以未能陈述诉因为由驳回……

对本案中的Flight Services International行使对人管辖权将违反正当程序，因为该公司既不存在一般管辖权也不存在对佛罗里达州的特定管辖权……

原告首先主张Atlas Air创造了对其宗教信仰敌意的工作环境，违反了《民权法》第七章。他们称Atlas Air"反复试图强迫"他们接种疫苗构成了"不受欢迎的骚扰"。

要胜诉这一主张，"原告需要证明被告有歧视意图或动机"。但原告未指控任何表明Atlas Air意图歧视其宗教信仰的事实。事实上，诉状所述恰恰相反。根据其政策，Atlas Air豁免了声称真诚反对新冠疫苗的雇员的疫苗接种要求。作为替代，他们必须在工作时佩戴口罩并每月接受一次检测。

原告现在似乎主张这些便利安排也对其宗教敌意。这个论点的问题在于诉状中没有任何东西支持它。原告未声称对口罩或检测有任何宗教反对——只有政治和后勤方面的反对。他们说口罩是"功能上无用"的"政治象征"，"除了识别'异见'员工外没有其他目的"。原告对检测没有提出任何真正的问题，只是笼统地抱怨它带来了"沉重负担"。这些负担是什么，以及它们是否具有宗教性质，原告没有说明。他们也未声称获得宗教豁免的雇员比获得医疗豁免的雇员受到更差的对待。

无论人们对原告不喜欢口罩和检测的看法如何，他们未指控任何表明Atlas Air对其宗教信仰有敌意的事实。Atlas Air怀有歧视动机的指控不仅是结论性的，而且"极其难以置信。"……

原告还根据《联邦食品、药品和化妆品法》提出主张，声称Atlas Air非法要求其雇员接受经紧急使用授权的医疗产品。但他们无权提起此类执行诉讼；只有联邦政府才能根据该法规提起执行诉讼……

原告还提出了几项州侵权诉因。第一项是Atlas Air通过向负责执行Covid-19协议的公司管理人员披露私人医疗信息（即疫苗接种状态）而侵权性地侵犯了他们的隐私。"在佛罗里达州，除身体侵犯的情况外，侵犯隐私的侵权行为必须伴随着向公众或大量人员公开。"而公开"要求某一事项通过向公众传播或向如此多的人传播而被公开，以至于该事项必须被视为必然成为公众知悉的事项。"原告在这一方面未指控足够的具体事实……

我们也驳回原告的故意造成精神损害主张。这需要证明Atlas Air故意或轻率地通过"极端和过分的行为"造成"严重精神损害"。被质疑的行为必须"超越一切可能的体面界限，并被视为残暴的，在一个文明社会中完全无法容忍。"

像当时许多其他雇主一样，Atlas Air要求采取某些预防措施以防止Covid-19的传播。但原告不同意Atlas Air采取的措施这一事实并不能使航空公司的行为超越一切可能的体面界限。地区法院正确地驳回了原告的故意造成精神损害主张。

文章《佛罗里达州政治家（安东尼·F·萨巴蒂尼）因简状中的人工智能臆造引用而受到批评》首次出现在Reason.com上。
articles.summary:
From Akerlund v. Atlas Air, Inc., decided yesterday by the Eleventh Circuit, Judge Britt Grant, joined by Judge Robin Rosenbaum and Embry Kidd:

A group of plaintiffs, employees in the commercial aviation business, personally reject their companies' pandemic-era policies on masks, testing, and vaccination. The district court dismissed the third amended complaint for lack of personal jurisdiction and failure to state a claim, and we affirm.

The claims in this case are remarkably weak, at least as pleaded. We are more candid than usual in this assessment because the plaintiffs' counsel Anthony F. Sabatini has not been candid with us. Sabatini filed multiple briefs replete with fake and hallucinated citations. Even after being warned. "Always a bad idea." Chief Justice John G. Roberts, Jr., 2023 Year-End Report on the Federal Judiciary, at 6 (2023). By outsourcing his legal work to an AI algorithm, Sabatini violated his ethical duties to both his clients and this Court….

Though the filings in this case were substandard in a variety of ways [I excerpt some of the substantive analysis below -EV], we have saved the worst for last. The plaintiffs' counsel Anthony F. Sabatini's filings are riddled with citations to nonexistent, "hallucinated" cases. His opening brief relies on at least eight such cases, including one purportedly decided by this Court.

After the defendants identified this problem, Sabatini acknowledged in his (untimely) proposed reply brief that those citations were "erroneous or unverifiable," and sought to withdraw his reliance on eight listed cases. At this point, things go from bad to worse: the eight cases Sabatini "withdrew" did not match a single one of the eight hallucinated cases in his opening brief. And not only were they not the right cases—all eight were also hallucinated.

We are far from the first court to see lawyers uncritically rely on artificial intelligence software and submit briefs citing nonexistent cases. The typical response when this kind of violation is identified is some version of an apology from the attorney, often with sanctions to follow. It is rare to see the kind of blatant and repeated misconduct that Sabatini has committed in this case.

The first rule of our profession is that a lawyer "shall provide competent representation to a client"—"competent" in the sense that it requires "legal knowledge, skill, thoroughness and preparation." It goes without saying that completely outsourcing one's legal work to artificial intelligence software is not competent. Doing so is a dramatic violation of the client's interests.

But it does not stop there, because lawyers are also officers of the Court. We expect that when lawyers submit briefs, they give us their best view of what the law is, and how that law supports their clients. We read those briefs carefully—not just because they are the product of counsel's time, effort, and skill, but because they help us as we try to reach the right answer. All that is lost if a lawyer decides that it is not worthwhile to do the work to persuade us—if he is willing to sign his name on whatever a machine churns out without so much as checking it for accuracy. We cannot do our job the same way unless lawyers do theirs.

Whatever the merits of artificial intelligence, it is no substitute for actual intelligence. Any "use of AI requires caution and humility." When lawyers rely on AI tools, there is no way to get around the obligation to verify that the software got it right, and that what it got wrong will not end up in court filings.

{AI algorithms are notorious for producing "outputs that echo users' opinions and beliefs, even when those views are incorrect." This tendency presents a particular danger in legal filings, as one of a lawyer's chief duties is to give his clients a clear-eyed view of whether the law says what the client wants it to say.}

By signing his name on briefs filled with hallucinated citations—not once but twice—Sabatini violated his professional obligations, both to his clients and to this Court. If he thinks these claims are not worth the effort, he should either tell his clients they fall short or advise them to hire another lawyer—not file obviously insufficient pleadings and briefs generated by AI tools. In a separate order, this Court, through the Chief Judge, will refer the matter to the Committee on Lawyer Qualifications and Conduct.

Orlando Sentinel (Annie Martin) reports that Sabatini is an elected County Commissioner for Lake County, Florida, and a former state representative:

The 37-year-old has made headlines for years for his controversial and often insulting social media posts and was warned by Republican leaders in Tallahassee to tone down his remarks even before he even attended his first legislative session in 2019.

In the past, he has tweeted a picture of an AR-15 targeted at George Floyd protesters, called for ending gay marriage and, when he ran unsuccessfully for the U.S. House of Representatives four years ago, posted on X, "I'm running for Congress to imprison as many Democrats as possible."

And a few excerpts related to the substance:

Atlas Air is a commercial airline, and Flight Services International hires contractors to staff Atlas Air's flights. During the Covid-19 pandemic, both companies required employees to vaccinate against Covid-19, unless they obtained a religious or medical exemption—in which case, they had to undergo periodic testing and wear a mask on the job.

The plaintiffs say they object on religious grounds to what they perceive as "a dangerous social and medical experiment." They explain that, among other things, their "conscience prohibits them from being inoculated with any experimental foreign substance," and that their religious rights were violated as a result of "the Biden Administration's goal of achieving universal vaccination and to unlawfully acquire [their] personal, genetic information." And in their view, the companies' accommodations for religious objectors to the vaccination requirement were unreasonable: monthly testing imposed "substantial burdens," and masks were "functionally useless" "symbols" that accomplished "nothing more than political advertising." Some plaintiffs say that they "succumbed to the pressure" and took the vaccine; others begrudgingly wore masks and tested. The complaint does not allege that anyone lost their job, but does allege that some plaintiffs were assigned to less desirable, lower-paying flights.

The plaintiffs assert that their employers' Covid-19 protocols led to a hostile work environment under Title VII; a Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act violation; a federal constitutional deprivation; a tortious invasion of privacy; a negligent disclosure of private medical information; and an infliction of emotional distress. The district court dismissed all claims against Flight Services International and some claims against Atlas Air for lack of personal jurisdiction, and the remaining claims against Atlas Air for failure to state a claim….

Exercising personal jurisdiction over Flight Services International in this lawsuit would violate due process because neither general nor specific jurisdiction is available in Florida for this company….

The plaintiffs first allege that Atlas Air created a work environment hostile to their religious beliefs, in violation of Title VII. They say that Atlas Air's "repeated attempts to coerce" them into getting vaccinated constituted "unwelcomed harassment."

To prevail on this claim, "the plaintiff is required to prove that the defendant had a discriminatory intent or motive." But the plaintiffs do not allege any facts indicating that Atlas Air intended to discriminate against their religious beliefs. In fact, the allegations suggest the opposite. Under its policy, Atlas Air exempted from its vaccine requirement employees who professed a sincere religious objection to the Covid-19 vaccine. Instead, they had to wear a mask at work and test once a month.

The plaintiffs now seem to assert that these accommodations are also hostile to their religion. The problem with this argument is that there is nothing to back it up in the pleadings. The plaintiffs do not allege any religious objections to masks or tests—only political and logistical ones. Masks, they say, are "functionally useless" "political symbols" that "serve no other purpose than identifying 'dissident' employees." And the plaintiffs offer no real problem with testing, other than a generalized complaint that it entails "substantial burdens." What those burdens are, and whether they are religious in nature, the plaintiffs do not say. Nor do they allege that employees who received religious exemptions were treated any worse than those who received medical exemptions.

Whatever one makes of the plaintiffs' dislike of masks and tests, they have alleged no facts signaling animosity from Atlas Air toward their religious beliefs. The allegation that Atlas Air harbored a discriminatory motive is not only conclusory, but "wildly implausible." …

The plaintiffs also bring claims under the Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act, alleging that Atlas Air unlawfully required its employees to take medical products authorized for emergency use. But they cannot sue to enforce such claims; only the federal government can bring enforcement actions under that statute….

The plaintiffs also allege several state tort claims. The first is that Atlas Air tortiously invaded their privacy by disclosing private medical information—namely, vaccination status—to company administrators in charge of enforcing Covid-19 protocols. "In Florida, except in cases of physical invasion, the tort of invasion of privacy must be accompanied by publication to the public in general or to a large number of persons." And publicity "requires that a matter be made public, by communicating it to the public at large, or to so many persons that the matter must be regarded as substantially certain to become one of public knowledge." The plaintiffs do not allege sufficient facts on this score….

We also reject the plaintiffs' intentional infliction of emotional distress claims. Those would require showing that Atlas Air intentionally or recklessly caused "severe emotional distress" through "extreme and outrageous conduct." The challenged conduct must "go beyond all possible bounds of decency, and to be regarded as atrocious, and utterly intolerable in a civilized community."

Like many other employers at the time, Atlas Air required certain precautions designed to prevent the spread of Covid-19. But the fact that the plaintiffs disagree with the steps Atlas Air took does not put the airline's actions beyond all possible bounds of decency. The district court correctly dismissed the plaintiffs' intentional infliction of emotional distress claims.

The post Florida Politician (Anthony F. Sabatini) Faulted for AI Hallucinations in Briefs appeared first on Reason.com.
